summary: Spolsky addresses the apparent redundancy of launching new Stack Exchange sites that cover topics already served by existing communities, using WordPress as a case study. He argues that there's no rule requiring only one Q&A site per topic, and that Stack Exchange's technology provides a fundamentally better experience than traditional forums. The post highlights specific advantages like voting, reputation systems, wiki-style editing, and lower barriers to participation. He predicts these features will eventually become standard everywhere, but until then Stack Exchange will continue expanding.
key_points: New Stack Exchange sites deliberately duplicate existing communities' subject matter, and that's fine because there's no rule limiting one Q&A site per topic
Stack Exchange's core advantages over traditional forums are voting, reputation, wiki-style editing, and lower participation barriers
Stack Overflow succeeded not by finding uncovered topics but by providing better technology for the same topics
Network effects naturally consolidate activity onto the better platform, as demonstrated by jQuery Q&A migrating to Stack Overflow organically
money_quotes: There's no rule that says that there needs to be exactly one Q&A website per topic.
WordPress.org's forums don't have voting, so you have to read through every answer and decide for yourself which one might solve your problem.
They don't have reputation, so there's no way to see whether you're getting an answer from someone who knows what they're talking about.
Stack Overflow is simply better than traditional forums, which is why it largely replaced proprietary forums.
Ultimately it didn't matter that much, because most of the jQuery Q&A activity happens on Stack Overflow anyway.
One day, the features that are standard on Stack Exchange will be copied everywhere.
key_insight: Winning in Q&A isn't about finding unserved topics — it's about providing superior technology for topics already being discussed elsewhere.
